elements.sent-2411-511: We take 650 questions from materials science question answering dataset (MaScQA)\cite{Zaki2024 , which required undergraduate-level understanding to solve them.}
elements.sent-2411-512: The authors classified them into four types based on their structure: Multiple choice questions (MCQs), Match the following type questions (MATCH), Numerical questions where options are given (MCQN), and numerical questions (NUM): see \autoref{mascqa_dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-513: MCQs are generally conceptual, given four options, out of which mostly one is correct, and sometimes more than one option is also correct.
elements.sent-2411-514: In MATCH, two lists of entities are given, which are to be matched with each other.
elements.sent-2411-515: These questions are also provided with four options, out of which one has the correct set of matched entities.
elements.sent-2411-516: In MCQN, the question has four choices, out of which the correct one is identified after solving the numerical problem stated in the question.
elements.sent-2411-517: The NUM type questions have numerical answers, rounded to the nearest integer or floating-point number as specified in the questions.
elements.sent-2411-518: To understand the performance of LLMs from a domain perspective, the questions were classified into 14 topical categories \cite{Zaki2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-519: The database can be accessed at \url{https://github.com/M3RG-IITD/MaScQA .}
elements.sent-2411-520: Our objective was to automate the evaluation of both open-source and proprietary LLMs on materials science questions from the MaScQA dataset and provide an interactive interface for students to solve these questions.
elements.sent-2411-521: We evaluated the performance of several language models, including LLAMA3-8B, HAIKU, SONNET, GPT-4, and OPUS, across the 14 various categories such as characterization, applications, properties, and behavior.. The evaluation involved:.
elements.sent-2411-522: Extracting corresponding values: For multiple-choice questions, correct answer options were extracted using regular expressions to compare model predictions against the correct choices.
elements.sent-2411-523: Prediction verification: For numerical questions, the predicted value was checked against a specified range or exact value.
elements.sent-2411-524: For multiple-choice questions, the predicted answer was verified against the correct option or the extracted corresponding value.
elements.sent-2411-525: Calculating accuracy: Accuracy was calculated for each question type and topic, and the overall accuracy across all questions was computed.

elements.sent-2411-534: Authors: Ruijie Zhu, Faradawn Yang, Andrew Qin, Suraj Sudhakar, Jaehee Park, Victor Chen.
elements.sent-2411-535: In the academic realm, researchers frequently present their work and that of others to colleagues and lab members.
elements.sent-2411-536: This task, while essential, is fraught with difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-537: For example, below are three challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-538: Reading and understanding research papers: Comprehending the intricacies of a research paper can be daunting, particularly for interdisciplinary subjects like materials science.
elements.sent-2411-539: Creating presentation slides: Designing slides that effectively communicate the content requires significant effort, including remaking slides, sourcing images, and determining optimal text and image placement.
elements.sent-2411-540: Tailoring to the audience: Deciding on the appropriate level of technical vocabulary and the number of slides needed to fit within a given time limit adds another layer of complexity.
elements.sent-2411-541: These challenges can be effectively addressed using large language models, which can streamline and automate the text summarization and slide creation process.
elements.sent-2411-542: LLMy Way leverages the power of GPT-3.5-turbo to automate the creation of academic slides from research articles.
elements.sent-2411-543: The methodology involves primarily three steps:.
elements.sent-2411-544: We prompt the large language model to generate a structured summary of the research paper, adhering to the typical sections of an academic paper: background, challenge, current status, method, result, conclusion, and outlook.
elements.sent-2411-545: Each section is summarized in under a certain number of words to ensure brevity and clarity.
elements.sent-2411-546: Example Prompt: Read the paper, and summarize in \{num\_words\} words each about the following:\textbackslash n\textbackslash n1.. Background\textbackslash n2.
elements.sent-2411-550: To create slides, we format the language model's output in a specific manner, using symbols to denote slide breaks.
elements.sent-2411-551: This output is then parsed and converted into a Markdown file, where additional images and text formatting are applied as needed.
elements.sent-2411-552: The formatted Markdown file is subsequently transformed into PDF slides using Marp.. Example output formatting:.
elements.lstlisting-2411-002: # Background Summary of the background here. --- # Challenge Summary of the challenge here. ---
elements.subsection-2411-037: Customization for Audience and Time Limit
elements.sent-2411-553: LLMy Way allows customization based on the target audience's expertise level (expert or non-expert) and the presentation time limit (e.g., 10 minutes, 15 minutes).
elements.sent-2411-554: This information is incorporated into the initial generation phase, ensuring that the content and slide count are appropriately tailored.
elements.sent-2411-555: This feature is to be implemented.
elements.sent-2411-556: LLMy Way represents a significant step forward towards the automation of academic presentation preparation.
elements.sent-2411-557: By leveraging the structured nature of scientific papers and the capabilities of advanced large language models, our tool addresses common pain points faced by researchers.
elements.sent-2411-558: The current implementation of our framework can be summarized into three consecutive steps.
elements.sent-2411-559: First, the research paper is parsed by LLM, which is summarized into predefined sections.
elements.sent-2411-560: Next, the summarized texts are converted into Markdown.
elements.sent-2411-561: Finally, Marp is used to generate the final slide deck in the PDF format.
elements.sent-2411-562: The current implementation of our framework uses GPT-3.5-turbo, but it can be adapted to other language models as needed.
elements.sent-2411-563: We also support the output format of LaTex to fit the needs of many researchers.
elements.sent-2411-564: Future work will focus on further refining the tool, incorporating user feedback, and exploring additional customization options.
elements.section-2411-020: WaterLLM: Creating a Custom ChatGPT for Water Purification Using Prompt-Engineering Techniques
elements.sent-2411-567: Drinking water pollution is a growing environmental problem that, ironically, comes from an increase in industrial production of new materials and chemicals.
elements.sent-2411-568: Common pollutants include heavy metals, perfluorinated compounds, microplastic particles, excreted medicinal drugs from hospitals, agricultural runoff and many others \cite{Mishra2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-569: The communities that suffer the most from water contamination often lack the plumbing infrastructure necessary for centralized water analysis and treatment.
elements.sent-2411-570: Decentralized and localized water treatments, based on resources available to the communities, can alleviate the problem.
elements.sent-2411-571: Since the resources can vary greatly, from well equipped analytical facilities to low-cost DIY solutions, a knowledge base that can rapidly provide information relevant for specific situations is needed.
elements.sent-2411-572: Here we show a prototype Large Language Model (LLM) chatbot that can take a variety of inputs about possible contaminants (ranging from detailed LC/MS analysis to general description of the situation) and propose the best solution to the water treatment for the particular case based on contaminant composition, cost and resources availability.
elements.sent-2411-573: We employed a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) capability of ChatGPT to answer questions about possible water treatments based on the latest scientific literature .
elements.sent-2411-574: For this project, we focused on advanced oxidation procedures (AOPs) for water purification from microplastics (MPs).
elements.sent-2411-575: In recent times, MPs have received significant attention globally due to their widespread presence in various species’ bodies, environmental media, and even bottled drinking water, as frequently documented \cite{Li2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-576: Numerous trials have been conducted and reported on the use of AOPs for breaking down diverse persistent microplastics as wastewater treatment methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-577: However, there remains a lack of guidelines on selecting the most suitable and cost effective treatment method based on the characteristics of the contaminant, maximum removal percentage of MP.
elements.sent-2411-578: The complexity of existing research on AOPs for MPs can be tackled with LLMs enhanced with RAG.
elements.sent-2411-579: RAG allows to augment LLM’s knowledge and achieve state of the art results on knowledge-intensive tasks.
elements.sent-2411-580: One straightforward modern way to implement LLM with RAG is through configuring a custom chatGPT.
elements.sent-2411-581: We uploaded current scientific papers under the “Knowledge” for RAG and tailored chatbot performance with prompt-engineering techniques such as grounding, context, and chain-of-thought reasoning to ensure that it delivers accurate, detailed, and useful information.
elements.sent-2411-582: To make sure that chatbot provides accurate and scientifically valid answers, we loaded the latest research on microplastic pollution remediation for RAG and implemented grounding in chat prompt.
elements.sent-2411-583: To collect the data, we gathered the initial set of 10 review articles from the expert in the field that talk about water purification using Advanced Oxidation Process.
elements.sent-2411-584: Then, we manually found 112 scientific articles discussing the specific treatment procedure.
elements.sent-2411-585: This way, our dataset has studies on different pollution sources like laundry, hospitals, industry, different pollutant types and their descriptive characteristics like size, shape, color, and different treatment methods like Ozonation, Fenton Reaction, UV, Heat-Activated Persulfate.
elements.sent-2411-586: We merged all papers into 8 pdfs to meet chatGPT “Knowledge” restrictions in files number, size and length.
elements.sent-2411-587: With grounding, we aim to anchor the chatbot's responses in concrete and factual knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-588: We explicitly asked chatGPT to avoid giving wordy broad generalized answers and to provide concise scientific details using the files uploaded under Knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-589: We provided context for the chatbot to understand and respond appropriately to user queries.
elements.sent-2411-590: We specified that Chatbot has expert-level knowledge on MPs and water purification strategies from MPs and other contaminants.
elements.sent-2411-591: We defined a user as a technician with basic knowledge on chemical engineering that needs to choose and apply a purification method.
elements.sent-2411-592: We set that the communication between Chatbot and User should be in the form of interactive dialog.
elements.sent-2411-593: It means that Chatbot should ask follow-up questions from the user and should finally return an accurate purification protocol with all the details that can be reproduced in the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-594: To further ensure that the chatbot considers all necessary aspects before providing a solution, we employed the chain-of-thought reasoning by breaking down the problem-solving process into sequential steps: Get the source of contamination.
elements.sent-2411-595: Ask what the pollutant particle is and suggest evaluation tests if it is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-596: Ask if the characteristics of pollutants are known: size, shape, type.
elements.sent-2411-597: Suggest the most effective purification approach that will get rid of the largest percentage of the pollutants and estimate the price.
elements.sent-2411-598: Adjust if it is too expensive.
elements.sent-2411-600: If unsatisfactory, go to the previous step.
elements.sent-2411-601: End of conversation: Chatbot should provide a table with all used purification methods and their parameters for established contaminants.
elements.sent-2411-602: These techniques allowed to boost the custom GPT performance, and WaterLLM demonstrated performance approved by the expert in the field.

elements.section-2411-021: yeLLowhaMMer: A Multi-modal Tool-calling Agent for Accelerated Research Data Management
elements.sent-2411-604: Bocarsly As scientific data continues to grow in volume and complexity, there is a great need for tools that can simplify the job of managing this data to draw insights, increase reproducibility, and accelerate discovery.
elements.sent-2411-605: Digital systems of record, such as electronic lab notebooks (ELN) or laboratory information management systems (LIMS), have been a great advancement in this area.
elements.sent-2411-606: However, complex tasks are still often too laborious, or simply impossible, to accomplish using graphical user interfaces alone, and any barriers to streamlined data management often lead to lapses in data recording.
elements.sent-2411-607: As developers of the open-source datalab \cite{Evans2024a ELN/LIMS, we explored how large language models (LLMs) can be used to simplify and accelerate data handling tasks in order to generate new insights, improve reproducibility, and save time for researchers.}
elements.sent-2411-608: Previously, we made progress toward this goal by developing a conversational assistant, named Whinchat \cite{Jablonka2023 , that allows users to ask questions about their data.}
elements.sent-2411-609: However, this assistant was unable to take action with a user’s data.
elements.sent-2411-610: Here, we developed yeLLowhaMmer, a multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based data management agent capable of taking free-form text and image instructions from users and executing a variety of complex scientific data management tasks.
elements.sent-2411-611: Our agent is powered by a low-cost commercial MLLM (Anthropic’s Claude 3 Haiku) used within a custom agentic infrastructure that allows it to write and execute Python code that interacts with datalab instances via the datalab-api package.
elements.sent-2411-612: In typical usage, a yeLLowhaMmer user might instruct the agent: “Pull up my 10 most recent sample entries and summarize the synthetic approaches used.” In this case, the agent will attempt to write and execute Python code using the datalab API to query for the user’s samples in the datalab instance and write a human-readable summary.
elements.sent-2411-613: If the code it generates gives an error (or does not give sufficient information), the agent can iteratively rewrite the program until the task is accomplished successfully.
elements.sent-2411-614: Furthermore, we leveraged the powerful multimodal capabilities of the latest MLLMs to allow for prompts that include visual cues.

elements.sent-2411-617: Notably, we found that even the inexpensive, fast model we used (Claude 3 Haiku) was able to perform sufficiently well at this task, while larger models may be explored in the future to allow for more advanced applications (though with slower speed and greater cost).
elements.sent-2411-618: We believe the capabilities demonstrated by yeLLowhaMmer show that MLLM agents have the potential to greatly lower the barrier to advanced data handling in experimental materials and chemistry laboratories.

elements.sent-2411-620: The codebox-api Python package was used to set up a local code sandbox that the agent has access to in order to safely read and save files, install Python packages, and run the code generated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-621: The datalab-api Python package was used to interact with datalab instances.
elements.sent-2411-622: An MLLM-compatible tool was designed to allow the model to use function-calling capabilities, write, and execute code within the sandbox.
elements.sent-2411-623: LangChain was used as a framework to interact with the commercial MLLM APIs and build the agentic loop.
elements.sent-2411-624: Streamlit was used to build a responsive GUI to show the conversation and upload/download the files from the codebox.
elements.sent-2411-625: A customized Streamlit callback was written to display the code and files generated by the agent in a user-friendly manner.
elements.sent-2411-626: An interesting challenge in the development of yeLLowhaMmer was the creation of a system prompt that would enable the agent to reliably generate robust code using the datalab-api package, which is a recent library not included in the training of the commercial models at the time of writing.
elements.sent-2411-627: Initially, we copied the existing documentation for the datalab-api into the system prompt, but we found that the code generated by the model was not working very well.
elements.sent-2411-628: Instead, it was helpful to produce a simplified version of the documentation that removed extraneous information and gave a few concrete examples of scripts.
elements.sent-2411-629: Additionally, we provided an abridged version of the datalab schemas in the JSON Schema format in the system prompt, which was necessary for the generation of compliant data to be inserted into datalab.
elements.sent-2411-630: Overall, the yeLLowhaMmer system prompt amounts to around 12,000 characters (corresponding to about 3200 tokens using Claude’s tokenizer).
elements.sent-2411-631: Given the large context windows of the current generation of MLLMs (e.g., 200k tokens for Claude 3 Haiku), this size of prompt is feasible for fast, extended conversations involving text, generated code, and images.
elements.sent-2411-632: In the future, we envision that library maintainers may wish to increase the utility of their libraries by maintaining two parallel sets of documentation: the standard human-readable documentation, and an abridged agents.txt (or llms.txt -- \url{https://llmstxt.org/ ) file that can be used by ML agents to write high-quality code using that library.}
elements.sent-2411-633: Going forward, we will undertake further prototyping to incorporate MLLM-based agents more tightly into our data management workflows, and to ensure that data curated or modified by such an agent will be appropriately `credited' by, for example, visually demarcating AI-generated content, and providing UI pathways to verify or `relabel' such data in an efficient manner.
elements.sent-2411-634: Finally, we emphasize the great progress made within the last year in MLLMs themselves, which are now able to handle audio and video content in addition to text and images.
elements.sent-2411-635: These will allow MLLM agents to use audiovisual data in real-time to provide new user interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-636: Based on these promising developments, we believe that data management platforms are well-placed to help bridge the divide from physical to digital data recording.
elements.sent-2411-638: Ladines, Vikrant Chaudhary Parsing raw data into a structured (meta)data standard or schema is a major Research Data Management (RDM) topic.. While defining F.A.I.R.
elements.sent-2411-639: This is typically done in two ways:.
elements.sent-2411-640: Fill a schema manually using electronic or physical lab notebooks, or.
elements.sent-2411-641: Create scripts that read the input/output raw files and parse them into the data schema.
elements.sent-2411-642: The first option is used in a lab setting where data is entered in real-time as it is generated.
elements.sent-2411-643: The second option is used when data files are available, albeit in an incompatible format to fill the schema directly.
elements.sent-2411-644: These can be measurement files coming from instruments or files generated from simulations.
elements.sent-2411-645: Specific parsers for each raw file type can transfer large amounts of data into schemas, making them essential for automation and big-data management.
elements.sent-2411-646: However, implementing parsers for all the possible raw files to a fill schema can be laborious and time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-647: It requires expert knowledge of the structure of the raw files and regular maintenance to keep up with new versions of raw files.
elements.sent-2411-648: In this work, we attempted to substitute parsers with Large Language Models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-649: We investigated whether LLMs can be used to parse data into structured schemas, thus relieving the need for coding parsers.
elements.sent-2411-650: As an example, we used raw files from X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements from three different instrument vendors (Bruker, Rigaku, and Panalytical).
elements.sent-2411-651: We defined a Pydantic model for our structured data schema and used the pre-trained Mixtral-8x7b from Groq.
elements.sent-2411-652: The data schemas are provided to the LLM using the function-calling mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-653: The schema is constructed by defining a Pydantic base model class and their fields or attributes with well-defined types and descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-654: The LLM tries to extract data for each variable from the raw files that matches these descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-655: Considering the size of the raw files and the token limitation of LLMs, we decided to create the following workflow:.
elements.sent-2411-656: Break the raw file contents into chunks.
elements.sent-2411-657: Prompt the LLM with the initial chunk.
elements.sent-2411-658: Generate a response from the LLM and populate the schema.
elements.sent-2411-659: Prompt the LLM with the next chunk along with the previous response.
elements.sent-2411-660: We found that when populating the schema, the LLM was correctly extracting the values in cases where the data types were float and str.
elements.sent-2411-661: This was the case for the XRDSettings class.
elements.sent-2411-662: However, when parsing values with a data type list[float], the LLM was often unable to extract the data in the expected format.
elements.sent-2411-663: This occurred when populating XRDResults class with the intensities data.
elements.sent-2411-664: The LLM output included non-numeric characters like \textbackslash n or \textbackslash t, along with hallucinated data values.
elements.sent-2411-665: By providing the previous response along with the new chunk of data in the prompts, we incorporated some degree of context.
elements.sent-2411-666: We found that using smaller chunk sizes led to the rapid replacement of the populated data.
elements.sent-2411-667: Sometimes, the correct data was replaced by hallucinated values.
elements.sent-2411-668: We used LangChain to build our models and prompt generators.
elements.sent-2411-669: The code is openly available on Github: \url{https://github.com/ka-sarthak/llmads .}
elements.sent-2411-670: Our work uses prompt engineering and function-calling.
elements.sent-2411-671: Future work into tuning the model temperature and fine-tuning could be explored to combat hallucination.
elements.sent-2411-672: Our work also indicates a need for human intervention to verify if the schema was filled correctly and to correct it when necessary.
elements.sent-2411-673: Nevertheless, our prompting strategy proves to be a valuable tool as it manages to initialize the schema properly for non-vectorial fields, all at the minimal effort of providing a structured schema and the raw files.
elements.section-2411-023: NOMAD Query Reporter: Automating Research Data Narratives
elements.sent-2411-674: Authors: Nathan Daelman, Fabian Schöppach, Carla Terboven, Sascha Klawohn, Bernadette Mohr Materials science research data management (RDM) platforms and structured data repositories contain large numbers of entries, each composed of property-value pairs.
elements.sent-2411-675: Users query these repositories by specifying property-value pairs to find entries matching specific criteria.
elements.sent-2411-676: While this guarantees that all returned entries have at least the queried properties, they do not provide context or insights into the structure and variety of other data present in them.
elements.sent-2411-677: Traditionally, it is up to the data scientist to examine the returned properties and interpret the overall response.
elements.sent-2411-678: To assist with this task, we use a LLM to create context-aware reports based on the properties and their meanings.
elements.sent-2411-679: We build and tested our ``Query Reporter'' \cite{Scheidgen2024 prototype on the NOMAD \cite{NomadQueryReporter repository, which stores heterogeneous materials science data, including metadata on scientific methodologies, atomistic structures, and materials properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-680: We developed the NOMAD Query Reporter \cite{Scheidgen2024 as a proof-of-concept.}
elements.sent-2411-681: It fetches and analyzes entries and produces a summary of the used methodology and standout results.
elements.sent-2411-682: It does so in a scientific style, lending itself as the basis for a “methods” section in a journal article.
elements.sent-2411-683: Our agent uses a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach \cite{Gao2024b , which enriches an LLM’s knowledge of external DB data without performing retraining or fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-684: To allow its safe application on private and unpublished data, we use a self-hosted Ollama instance running Meta’s Llama3 70B \cite{Touvron2023 model.}
elements.sent-2411-685: We tested the agent on publicly available data from NOMAD.
elements.sent-2411-686: To manage Llama3’s context window of 8,000 tokens, the entries are collected as rows into a Pandas dataframe.
elements.sent-2411-687: Each row (e.g., entry) is individually passed on to the LLM via the chat-completion API.
elements.sent-2411-688: Instead of a single message, it accepts a multi-turn conversation that simulates several demarcated roles.
elements.sent-2411-689: We use the “system” and “user” roles of the chat to reinforce the retention of parts of the previous summary.
elements.sent-2411-690: This approach generally conforms to the Naive RAG category in Gao et al.’s classification \cite{Gao2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-692: We used two kinds of data for testing: (a) homogeneous, property-value pairs of computational data; and (b) heterogeneous text typed properties of solar cell experiments, often formatted as in-text tables.
elements.sent-2411-693: We engineered different prompts for each kind.
elements.sent-2411-694: The agent performed better on the homogeneous than the heterogeneous data.
elements.sent-2411-695: Here, summaries would often suffer from irrelevant threads, or even hallucinations.
elements.sent-2411-696: We theorize that homogeneous data maps more consistently onto our predefined dataframe columns, which aids the LLM in interpreting follow-up messages.
elements.sent-2411-697: Still, we could not improve the performance for heterogeneous data within the hackathon.
elements.sent-2411-698: In short, the NOMAD Query Reporter demonstrates that the combined approach of filtering and RAG can effectively summarize collections of limited-size, structured data directly stored in research data repositories, allowing for automated drafting of methods and setups for publications at a consistent level of quality and style.
elements.sent-2411-699: These results suggest applicability for other well-defined materials science APIs, such as the OPTIMADE standard \cite{Evans2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-700: Follow-up work includes investigating the impact of Advanced RAG strategies \cite{Gao2024b .}

Intermediate steps managing hallucinations or token limits are marked in red and orange, respectively. \label{fig:nomad_query_reporter}}
elements.section-2411-024: Speech-schema-filling: Creating Structured Data Directly from Speech
elements.sent-2411-702: Authors: Hampus Näsström, Julia Schumann, Michael Götte, José A.. Márquez.
elements.sent-2411-703: As the amount of materials science data being created increases, so do the efforts to make this data Findable, Accessible, Reusable, and Interoperable (FAIR) \cite{Wilkinson2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-704: One pragmatic approach to creating FAIR data is by defining so-called data schemas for the various types of data being recorded.
elements.sent-2411-705: These schemas can then be used in both input tools like electronic lab notebooks (ELNs) and storage solutions like data repositories to create structured data.
elements.sent-2411-706: One widely adopted standard for writing data schemas is the so-called JSON Schema \cite{JSON .}
elements.sent-2411-707: JSON Schema allows us to define objects, such as, for example, a solution preparation experiment in the lab, with properties such as temperature and a list of solutes and solvents (see \autoref{fig:nomad_json_schema a).}
elements.sent-2411-708: These schemas can then be used to create forms in an ELN like NOMAD \cite{Scheidgen2023 (see \autoref{fig:nomad_json_schema b).}}
elements.sent-2411-709: However, in a lot of lab situations, such as when working inside a glovebox, it is difficult to i) navigate the ELN and select the right form and ii) actually fill in the form with experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-710: In our experience, this usually results in users having to record their data later from memory or even in data not being recorded at all.
elements.sent-2411-711: We propose a solution for this using LLMs to:.
elements.sent-2411-712: Converting spoken language in the lab into text using advanced speech recognition technologies, such as OpenAI's Whisper.
elements.sent-2411-713: Based on the text select and fill the appropriate schema, enabling accurate data capture without manual text entry.
elements.sent-2411-714: The first step of converting speech into structured data is to record the speech and transcribe it into text.
elements.sent-2411-715: There are multiple options for recording audio, and the best solution depends on both the hardware and the operating system used.
elements.sent-2411-716: We use the Recognizer class from the Python package SpeechRecognition \cite{SpeechRecognition to perform the recording only, while leaving the actual speech recognition to OpenAIs Whisper.}
elements.sent-2411-717: There is an open version of this available for download through the Python package openai-whisper \cite{OpenaiWhisper .}
elements.sent-2411-718: Once the speech has been converted to text we need to use this text to i) select the appropriate schema and ii) fill the schema.
elements.sent-2411-719: For this, we make use of a common feature in LLMs called “function calling”, “API calling”, or “tool use”.
elements.sent-2411-720: This has been developed to allow LLMs to make valid API calls and, since OpenAPI is validated by it, uses JSON Schema to define the possible “functions”, or “tools”, that can be outputted.
elements.sent-2411-721: Since we use JSON Schema to define our data schemas we can simply supply these as the tools for the LLM to use.
elements.sent-2411-722: One way to do this in Python is to write a Pydantic schema for each of the data schemas and then convert this to a JSON Schema.
elements.sent-2411-723: For the Llama model we used, the python package langchain-experimental \cite{LangChainc has an OllamaFunctions class that can be imported from the llms.ollama\_functions sub-package.}
elements.sent-2411-724: This class can then be used to instantiate the model and add the valid data schemas.
elements.sent-2411-725: Here is an example using LangChain and Llama3:.
elements.lstlisting-2411-003: from langchain_experimental.llms.ollama_functions import OllamaFunctions model = OllamaFunctions(model="llama3:70b", base_url='...', format='json') model = model.bind_tools( tools=[ { "name": "solution_preparation", "description": "Schema for solution preparation", "parameters": SolutionPreparation.schema(), }, { "name": "powder_scaling", "description": "Schema for powder scaling", "parameters": Scaling.schema(), } ], )
elements.sent-2411-726: Where SolutionPreparation and Scaling are Pydantic models for our desired data schemas.
elements.sent-2411-727: Finally, this can be chained together with a prompt template and used to process the transcribed audio from before:.
elements.lstlisting-2411-004: prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(...) chain_with_tools = prompt | model response = chain_with_tools.invoke(transcribed_audio) For langchain the selected schema can be retrieved from: schema = response.additional_kwargs['function_call']['name'] And the filled instance from: instance = json.loads( response.additional_kwargs['function_call']['arguments'])
elements.sent-2411-728: For LangChain the selected schema can be retrieved from:.
elements.sent-2411-730: A detailed example of the text-to-structured data can be found as an iPython notebook on \url{github.com/hampusnasstrom/speech-schema-filling together with an implementation of the audio recording and transcribing using Whisper.}
elements.sent-2411-731: In conclusion, we believe that LLMs can be useful in labs where traditional ELNs are hard to operate by transcribing speech, selecting appropriate schemas, and filling the schemas to ultimately create structured data directly from speech.
elements.figure-2411-031: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{images/Picture17.png} \caption{a) Part of a JSON Schema defining a data structure for a solution preparation. b) The schema converted to an ELN form in NOMAD \cite{Scheidgen2023}. \label{fig:nomad_json_schema}}
elements.section-2411-025: Leveraging LLMs for Bayesian Temporal Evaluation of Scientific Hypotheses
elements.sent-2411-734: Science is predicated on empiricism, and a scientist uses the tools at their disposal to gather observations that support the veracity of their claims.
elements.sent-2411-735: When one cannot gather firsthand evidence for a claim, they must rely on their own assessment of evidence presented by others.
elements.sent-2411-736: However, the scientific literature on claim is often large and dense (particularly for those without domain expertise), and the scientific consensus on the veracity of a claim may drift over time.
elements.sent-2411-737: In this work we consider how large language models (LLMs), in conjunction with temporal Bayesian statistics, can rapidly provide a more holistic view of a scientific inquiry.
elements.sent-2411-738: We demonstrate our approach on the hypothesis that the material LK-99, which went viral after its discovery in April 2023, is in fact a room-temperature superconductor.
elements.sent-2411-739: Scientific progress requires a researcher to iteratively update their prior understanding based on new observations.
elements.sent-2411-740: The process of updating a statistical prior based on new information is the backbone of Bayesian statistics and is routinely used in scientific workflows \cite{Settles2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-741: Under such a model, a hypothesis has an inferred probability $P_H \in (0,1)$ of being true and will never be completely discarded ($P_H=0$) or accepted ($P_H=1$) but may draw arbitrarily close to these extrema.
elements.sent-2411-742: This inferred probability from a dataset is also a feature of assessing the power of a claim using statistical hypothesis tests \cite{Lehmann1986 , which are commonly used across most scientific disciplines.}
elements.sent-2411-743: Modelling the veracity of a scientific claim as a probability also stems from the work of philosopher Karl Popper \cite{Popper2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-744: Popper posits that a scientific claim should be falsifiable, such that it can be refuted by empirical evidence.
elements.sent-2411-745: If a scientific claim cannot be refuted, Popper argues that it is not proven, but gains further credibility from the scientific community.
elements.sent-2411-746: Scientific progress is not made by proving claims, but instead by hewing away false claims through observation until only those that withstand repeated scrutiny remain.
elements.sent-2411-747: The history of science is littered with discarded hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-748: Some of these claims were believed to be true for centuries before being dismissed (including geocentrism, phrenology, energeticism, and spontaneous generation).
elements.sent-2411-749: In this work, we focus on a claim by Lee, Kim, and Kwon that they had successfully synthesized a room-temperature superconductor, which they called LK-99 \cite{Lee2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-750: Such a material would necessitate altering the existing theory of superconductors at a fundamental level and would enable innovations that are currently beyond reach.
elements.sent-2411-751: LK-99 went viral in summer 2023 \cite{Chang2023 , but replication efforts quickly ran into issues \cite{Garisto2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-752: Since the initial claim was made in April 2023, roughly 160 works have been published, and the scientific consensus now appears established: LK-99 is not a room-temperature superconductor.
elements.sent-2411-753: Our dataset consists of the 160 papers on Google Scholar, ordered by publication date, that are deemed relevant to the hypothesis ``LK-99 is a room-temperature superconductor.'' For each paper abstract in our dataset, we construct an LLM prompt as follows to perform a zero-shot natural language inference operation \cite{Liu2021 : \begin{displayquote Given a Hypothesis and an Abstract, determine whether the Hypothesis is an ‘entailed’, ‘neutral’, or ‘contradicted’ by the Abstract.}}
elements.sent-2411-754: Next, we constructed a temporal Bayesian model where, starting from an initial Gaussian prior $N(\mu_P,\sigma_P^2)$ that models the likelihood of accepting the hypothesis, we can update with a Gaussian likelihood $N(\mu_L,\sigma_L^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-755: Our likelihood is a Gaussian designed so that for a given publication, “entailment” acceptance probability higher, “contradiction” pushes the acceptance probability lower, and “neutrality” leaves the acceptance probability the same.
elements.sent-2411-756: Our likelihood probability is further weighted according to the impact factor of the journal, with an impact factor floor set to accommodate publications that have not passed peer review.
elements.sent-2411-757: Retroactively, we could also weight by the number of citations, however we omit this feature in our analysis since this is inherently a post-hoc metric and would violate our temporal assessment.
elements.sent-2411-758: We update our Gaussian prior using the equations \cite{Murphy2007 : \begin{equation}\mu_P \leftarrow \biggr( \frac{1}{\sigma_P^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_L^2} \biggr)^{-1} \biggr( \frac{\mu_P}{\sigma_P^2} + \frac{\mu_L}{\sigma_L^2} \biggr);\; \sigma_P^2 \leftarrow \biggr( \frac{1}{\sigma_P^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_L^2} \biggr)^{-1}\end{equation} We are also able to specify the initial probability associated with the hypothesis ($\mu_P$), as well as how flexible we are in changing our perspective based on new evidence ($\sigma_P^2$).}
elements.sent-2411-759: We select two initial probabilities: 50\% and 20\%, and fix standard deviations $\sigma_P^2$ and $\sigma_L^2$.
elements.sent-2411-760: Assuming either “contradiction” or “entailment”, the update due to $\mu_L$ then scales linearly with the publication impact factor.
elements.sent-2411-761: Finally, we can compare our temporal probability assessment with probabilities provided by the betting platform Manifold Markets \cite{ManifoldMarkets2024 , where players bet on the outcome of a successful replication of the LK-99 publication results.}
elements.sent-2411-762: We find that our temporal Bayesian probabilities, with an adjusted initial prior, can mirror the Manifold Markets probabilities with two interesting divergences.
elements.sent-2411-763: While the initial probability starts at around 20\% for both, the temporal Bayesian approach never goes above 30\%.
elements.sent-2411-764: By contrast, the betting market, following the hype and virality of the LK-99 publication, reaches a peak at around 60\%.
elements.sent-2411-765: Furthermore, while the betting market has a long tail of low probability starting in mid-August 2023, our approach more quickly disregards the hypothesis based on a continuing accumulation of studies showing that the LK-99 findings could not be replicated.
elements.sent-2411-766: Our temporal Bayesian model with the adjusted initial prior reaches a probability below 1\% by mid-September 2023, but never entirely dismisses the chance that the hypothesis is true.
elements.sent-2411-767: While we specifically select initial probabilities or 20\% and 50\%, both trajectories end with a steadily shrinking probability of accepting the hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-768: Our initial prior probability of 50\% mimics an unbiased observer with no knowledge about whether the hypothesis should be accepted or rejected.
elements.sent-2411-769: A prior probability of 20\% could be considered a reasonable guess for an observer biased by a baseline understanding and suspicion of the claims and their corresponding evidence.
elements.sent-2411-770: Such an initial guess is admittedly subjective, as is the degree to which new information affects one’s inherent biases.
elements.sent-2411-771: We treat these settings as presets, however these are trivial for others to configure and assess based on their background.
elements.sent-2411-772: Finally, for claims with established scientific consensus, our approach is guaranteed to asymptotically approach that consensus where the rate of convergence varies according to these initial presets.
elements.figure-2411-032: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{images/Picture14.png} \caption{Likelihood of accepting the hypothesis “LK-99 is a room-temperature superconductor” via three approaches, from April 15, 2023 to April 15, 2024. The unadjusted initial probability (set to 50\%) is shown in gray, the adjusted initial probability (set to 20\%) is shown in black, and the probability according to the Manifold Markets online betting platform is shown in red. \label{fig:LK99_temporal}}
elements.sent-2411-773: Carefully validating a claim based a body of scientific literature can be a time-consuming and challenging prospect, especially without domain expertise.
elements.sent-2411-774: In this work, we demonstrate how a claim might be evaluated using a temporal Bayesian model based on a literature evaluation using natural language inference.
elements.sent-2411-775: We show how our aggregated literature predictions allow us to quickly reject the hypothesis that LK-99 is a room-temperature superconductor.
elements.sent-2411-776: In the future, we hope to apply this approach to other scientific claims, including those with debates that are ongoing and as well as claims have an established scientific consensus.
elements.sent-2411-777: In general, we hope this approach will allow a researcher to quickly measure the scientific community’s confidence in a claim, as well as aid the public in assessing both the veracity of a claim and the change in confidence driven by continued experimentation and observation.
elements.section-2411-026: Multi-Agent Hypothesis Generation and Verification through Tree of Thoughts and Retrieval Augmented Generation
elements.sent-2411-778: Authors: Aleyna Beste Ozhan, Soroush Mahjoubi.
elements.sent-2411-779: Our project, developed during the “LLM Hackathon for Applications in Materials and Chemistry,” aims to accelerate scientific hypotheses and enhance the creativity of scientific inquiry.
elements.sent-2411-780: We propose using a multi-agent system of specialized large language models (LLMs) to streamline and enrich hypothesis generation and verification in materials science.
elements.sent-2411-781: This approach leverages diverse, fine-tuned LLM agents collaborating to generate and validate novel hypotheses more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-782: While similar pipelines have been proven useful in the social sciences \cite{Yang2023 , to the best of our knowledge, this work marks the first adaptation of such an approach to hypothesis generation in materials science.}
elements.sent-2411-783: As illustrated in \autoref{fig:multi_agent_hyp_gen , The system includes agents such as a background provider, an inspiration generator, a hypothesis generator, and three evaluators.}
elements.sent-2411-784: Each agent plays a crucial role in formulating and assessing hypotheses, ensuring only the most viable and compelling ideas are developed.
elements.sent-2411-785: This innovative approach fosters an environment conducive to scientific inquiries.
elements.sent-2411-786: The background extraction module is designed to search through a vast database for relevant information directly related to the user’s query.
elements.sent-2411-787: This module employs advanced embedding-based retrieval techniques to identify and extract pertinent corpus.
elements.sent-2411-788: As new papers and findings are added to the repository, the system dynamically updates, ensuring the use of the most current and relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-789: The inspiration generator agent leverages extensive background data to effectively formulate inspirations using a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-790: Serving as the strategic core of the hypothesis generation process, it draws inspiration from a broad spectrum of sources to spawn diverse hypotheses, similar to the branching structure of a "Tree of Thoughts (ToT)" \cite{Yao2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-791: The agent samples "k" candidates as possible solutions, evaluates their effectiveness through self-feedback, and votes on the most promising candidates.
elements.sent-2411-792: The selection is narrowed down to "b" promising options per step, with this structured approach helping the agent systematically refine its solutions.
elements.sent-2411-793: Based on the background information and the inspirations, this module generates meaningful research hypotheses.

elements.sent-2411-819: Humans have conducted material research for thousands of years, yet the vast chemical space, estimated to encompass up to 10\textsuperscript{60 compounds, remains unexplored.}
elements.sent-2411-820: Traditional research methods often focus on incremental improvements, making the discovery of new materials a slow process.
elements.sent-2411-821: As data infrastructure has developed, data mining techniques have increasingly accelerated material discovery.
elements.sent-2411-822: However, three significant obstacles hinder this process: First, the availability and sparsity of data present a major challenge.
elements.sent-2411-823: Comprehensive and high-quality datasets are essential for effective data mining, yet materials science suffer from limited data availability.
elements.sent-2411-824: Second, each database typically consists of specific types of quantitative properties, which may not fully meet researchers' needs.
elements.sent-2411-825: This fragmentation and specialization of databases can impede the holistic analysis necessary for breakthrough discoveries.
elements.sent-2411-826: Third, scientists usually focus on certain materials and related articles, decreasing the likelihood of deeply exploring diverse literature that reports potential materials or applications not yet utilized in their specific field.
elements.sent-2411-827: This siloed approach further limits the scope of discovery and innovation.
elements.sent-2411-828: Unlike the intrinsic properties found in databases, scientific articles provide unstructured but higher- level information, such as applications, material categories, and properties that might not be explicitly recorded in databases.
elements.sent-2411-829: Additionally, these texts include inferences and theories proposed by domain experts, which are crucial for guiding research directions.
elements.sent-2411-830: The challenge lies in automatically extracting and digesting this unstructured text into actionable insights.
elements.sent-2411-831: This process typically requires experienced experts, creativity, and a measure of luck to identify the next desirable candidate.
elements.sent-2411-832: These challenges motivate the potential of utilizing large language models to parse scientific reports and integrate the extracted information into knowledge graphs, thereby constructing high-level insights.

elements.sent-2411-852: Authors: Alexander Al-Feghali, Sylvester Zhang Large language models (LLMs) and foundation models have garnered significant attention lately due to their natural language programmability and potential to parse high-dimensional data from reactions to the scientific literature \cite{Jablonka2024c .}
elements.sent-2411-853: While these models have demonstrated utility in various chemical and materials science applications, we propose leveraging their designed strength in language processing to develop a first pass peer review system for materials science research papers \cite{Boiko2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-854: Traditional-gram tests such as BLEU or ROUGE, as well as X-of-thought LLM-based evaluations, are not sensitive enough for creativity or diversity in scientific writing \cite{Liu2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-855: Our approach utilizes the learned probabilistic features to establish a baseline for typical scientific language in materials science, based on fine-tuning on materials science abstracts through a historical train-test split.
elements.sent-2411-856: New abstracts are scored by their weighted-average probabilities, identifying those that deviate from the expected norms, flagging both possibly innovative or potentially nonsensical works.
elements.sent-2411-857: As a proof-of-concept in this direction, we fine-tuned two models: OPT (6.7B) and TinyLLama (1.1B) using the Huggingface PEFT library's Low Rank Adapters (LoRA) to access the log probabilities of the abstracts, which is not typically accessible for modern API services \cite{Mangrulkar2022, Zhang2024, Zhang2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-858: Our results come with the usual caveats for small models with small computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-859: We curated a dataset of 6000 abstracts from PubMed, published between 2017--2020, focusing on Materials Science and Chemistry \cite{NCBI2024 .}

elements.sent-2411-861: We compared highly cited papers ($>$200 citations) with those of average citation counts.

elements.section-2411-030: LithiumMind - Leveraging Language Models for Understanding Battery Performance
elements.sent-2411-891: Authors: Xinyi Ni, Zizhang Chen, Rongda Kang, Sheng-Lun Liao, Pengyu Hong, Sandeep Madireddy.
elements.sent-2411-892: In this project, we explore multiple applications of Large Language Models (LLMs) in analyzing the performance of lithium metal batteries.
elements.sent-2411-893: Central to our investigation is the concept of Coulombic efficiency (CE), a critical measure in battery technology that assesses the efficiency of electron transfer during a battery's charge and discharge cycles.
elements.sent-2411-894: Improving the CE is essential for advancing the adoption of high-energy-density lithium metal batteries, which are crucial for next-generation energy storage solutions.
elements.sent-2411-895: A key focus of our analysis is the role of the liquid electrolyte engineering, which is instrumental in determining the battery's cyclability and improving the CE.
elements.sent-2411-896: We explored two methods of integrating LLM as a supplemental tool in battery research.
elements.sent-2411-897: First, we utilize LLMs as information extractors to distill structural knowledge essential for the design of electrolytes from the vast amount of paper.
elements.sent-2411-898: Second, we introduce an LLM-powered chatbot specifically tailored to respond to inquiries related to lithium metal batteries.
elements.sent-2411-899: We believe these applications of LLMs may potentially enhance our capabilities in battery innovation, streamlining research processes and increasing the accessibility of specialized knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-901: In this project, we utilize LLM to extract CE-electrolyte pairs from over 70 papers.
elements.sent-2411-902: A recent study \cite{Dataset_S01 (Dataset S01) provides a reliable database about the relationship between Coulombic Efficiency (CE) and battery electrolytes, along with a list of relevant papers.}
elements.sent-2411-903: The data was filtered and cleaned by human experts.
elements.sent-2411-904: We managed to automate the data collection procedure through LithiumMind and aimed to discover more relationships from the paper list.
elements.sent-2411-905: The pipeline is describe in the left column of Figure 1.
elements.sent-2411-906: The pipeline consists of the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-907: Parse PDF: The raw papers were saved as PDF files and loaded into text using a PDF parser.
elements.sent-2411-908: Retrieve Relevant Context: Instead of extracting information directly from the whole paper, we ingest the documents into a vector datastore.
elements.sent-2411-909: We combined three domain-specific embedding models - MaterialBERT, ChemBERT, and OpenAI - together as a powerful retriever through the LangChain LOTR module.
elements.sent-2411-910: The retriever finds 10 text chunks for each paper that are most relevant to Coulombic Efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-911: CE Extraction: We defined the schema for extracting key information, including Coulombic Efficiency and the solvent and salt of the electrolyte.
elements.sent-2411-912: We provided few-shot in-context instruction to GPT-4 Turbo JSON mode.
elements.sent-2411-913: The extracted output is saved in JSON format.
elements.sent-2411-914: The LLM extracted 334 CE- electrolyte pairs in 71 papers, while the original paper found 152 pairs.
elements.sent-2411-915: Since it is difficult to verify our results without the help of human experts, we filtered the extracted pairs through the Coulombic Efficiency and found 46 matches to the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-916: We classified these verifiable data into three categories: correct (the extracted data exactly matches the human-labeled data); incorrect (the types or amounts of the solvent/salt do not match the labels); and unknown (the extracted data provides more details than the human-labeled data).
elements.sent-2411-917: For example, the label only shows the types of the salts, but the extracted data contains not only the correct types but also the mix ratio of different salts.
elements.sent-2411-919: One major challenge is the low recall of the extracted information, as only 46 out of 152 labeled pieces of information were retrieved.
elements.sent-2411-920: Upon investigating the papers, we found that much of the Coulombic Efficiency was recorded in tables and figures, which were dropped during PDF parsing.
elements.sent-2411-921: It is necessary to introduce multimodal LLMs to further investigate those papers.
elements.subsection-2411-053: Investigation 2: Advanced Chatbot for Lithium Battery Q/A
elements.sent-2411-922: In this exploration, we utilized a curated collection of research papers focused on lithium metal batteries to construct a vector database and developed a chatbot employing a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework.
elements.sent-2411-923: Our Q/A pipeline includes two types of answer strategies: General Q/A and Knowledge-graph-based Q/A.
elements.sent-2411-924: The pipeline is described in Figure 1.
elements.sent-2411-925: In this section, we detail our comprehensive Q/A pipeline designed for the exploration of lithium metal battery-related research.
elements.sent-2411-926: We began by sourcing and downloading 71 research papers pertinent to lithium metal batteries.
elements.sent-2411-927: The information extracted from these papers was encoded into vectors and stored in Chroma databases.
elements.sent-2411-928: To best reflect the specialized language of the field, we created two distinct databases: one utilizing MaterialsBERT for materials science content, and another using ChemBERT for chemical context.
elements.sent-2411-929: During the retrieval phase, we employ LOTR (MergerRetriever) to enhance the precision of document retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-930: Upon receiving a user query, the system retrieves relevant document segments from each database.
elements.sent-2411-931: It then removes any redundant results from the merged outputs and selects the top 10 most pertinent document chunks.
elements.sent-2411-932: Finally, both the selected context and the user query are processed by GPT-4 Turbo to generate an informed and accurate response.
elements.sent-2411-933: This pipeline exemplifies a robust integration of state-of-the-art technologies to facilitate advanced research interrogation and knowledge discovery in the domain of lithium metal batteries.
elements.sent-2411-934: We built a knowledge graph with the extracted information using Neo4j.
elements.sent-2411-935: The knowledge graph consists of four node types:.
elements.sent-2411-936: Solvent node: properties include name, SMILES, density, and weight.
elements.sent-2411-937: Salt node: properties include name, SMILES, and weight.
elements.sent-2411-938: Electrolyte node: properties include name and Coulombic Efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-939: Reference node: properties include title, content, and page number and,.
elements.sent-2411-940: The built knowledge graph can be accessed and visualized using the Neo4j web application.
elements.sent-2411-941: By using GraphCypherQAChain in LangChain, LLMs can generate Cypher queries to solve users' questions.
elements.sent-2411-942: This capability allows the LLMs to address user queries by filtering and leveraging relationships between data points, which is particularly valuable in complex domains such as lithium battery technology.
elements.sent-2411-943: This integration ensures that our RAG pipeline is adept at handling domain-specific questions and excels in scenarios where understanding the interconnections within data is crucial.
elements.section-2411-031: KnowMat: Transforming Unstructured Material Science Literature into Structured Knowledge
elements.sent-2411-945: Sayeed, Ramsey Issa, Trupti Mohanty, Taylor Sparks.
elements.sent-2411-946: The rapid growth of materials science has led to an explosion of scientific literature, often presented in unstructured formats that pose significant challenges for systematic data extraction and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-947: To address this, we developed KnowMat, a novel tool designed to transform complex, unstructured material science literature into structured knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-948: Leveraging advanced Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Llama 3, KnowMat automates the extraction of critical information from scientific texts, converting them into structured JSON formats.
elements.sent-2411-949: This tool not only enhances the efficiency of data processing but also facilitates deeper insights and innovation in material science research.
elements.sent-2411-950: KnowMat's user-friendly interface allows researchers to input material science papers, which are then parsed and analyzed to extract key insights.
elements.sent-2411-951: The tool's versatility in handling multiple input files and its capacity for customization through sub-field specific prompts make it an indispensable resource for researchers aiming to streamline their workflow.
elements.sent-2411-952: Additionally, KnowMat's ability to integrate with other tools and platforms, along with its support for various LLMs, ensures that it remains adaptable to the evolving needs of the research community.
elements.sent-2411-953: The KnowMat workflow begins with parsing unstructured text from material science literature using a tool called Unstructured \cite{Unstructured .}
elements.sent-2411-954: This tool reads the input file, separates out the sections, and stores everything in a machine-readable format.
elements.sent-2411-955: This initial step involves identifying relevant sections and extracting pertinent information related to material compositions, properties, and experimental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-956: KnowMat provides field-specific prompts for several fields, and it offers the flexibility for users to customize these prompts further or create their own prompts for new fields.
elements.sent-2411-957: This feature ensures that the extracted data is both relevant and comprehensive, tailored to the specific needs of the researcher.
elements.sent-2411-958: The interface allows users to define the scope and focus of the extraction process effectively.
elements.sent-2411-959: To enhance usability and interoperability, KnowMat supports seamless integration with other tools and platforms.
elements.sent-2411-960: Extracted results can be easily exported in CSV format, enabling straightforward data sharing and further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-961: The tool's flexibility extends to its compatibility with various LLMs, including both subscription-based models like GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 \cite{OpenaiModels , and open-source models like Llama 3 \cite{Llama3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-962: This ensures that researchers can select the most suitable LLM for their specific requirements.

The Large Language Model (LLM) powered by engineered prompts processes the reference text to extract key information, such as material composition, properties, and measurement conditions, and converts it into a structured JSON output. \label{fig:knowmat_workflow}}
elements.sent-2411-963: In initial tests, KnowMat demonstrated promising results in the efficiency and accuracy of data extraction from material science literature.
elements.sent-2411-964: The tool successfully parsed and structured information from multiple papers, converting complex textual data into actionable insights.
elements.sent-2411-965: For instance, in a study involving superconductors, KnowMat accurately extracted detailed information on compositions, critical temperatures, and experimental conditions, presenting them in a structured JSON format.
elements.sent-2411-966: Looking ahead, future developments for KnowMat include enhancing the editing capabilities for field-specific prompts, improving the handling of multiple input files in a single operation, and expanding output options to further enhance flexibility and usability.
elements.sent-2411-967: Continuous improvement and expansion of field-specific prompts will ensure that KnowMat remains a valuable tool for researchers across various domains of material science.
elements.sent-2411-968: In conclusion, KnowMat represents a significant advancement in the field of knowledge extraction from scientific literature.
elements.sent-2411-969: By converting unstructured material science texts into structured formats, it provides researchers with powerful tools to unlock insights and drive innovation in their fields.
elements.sent-2411-970: Authors: Qianxiang Ai, Jiaru Bai, Kevin Shen, Jennifer D'Souza, Elliot Risch.
elements.sent-2411-971: Organic synthesis is often described in unstructured text without a standard taxonomy, which makes synthesis data less searchable and less compatible with downstream data-driven tasks (e.g., retrosynthesis, condition recommendation) compared to structured records.
elements.sent-2411-972: The specificities and writing styles of these descriptions also vary, ranging from simple sentences about chemical transformations to long paragraphs that include procedure details.
elements.sent-2411-973: This leads to ambiguities, unidentified missing information, challenges in comparing different syntheses, and can impede proper experimental reproduction.
elements.sent-2411-974: In last year's hackathon, we fine-tuned an open-source LLM to extract data structured in the Open Reaction Database schema from synthesis procedures \cite{Ai2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-975: While this method has proved to be successful for patent text, it relies on existing unstructured-structured data pairs and does not generalize well to non-patent writing styles.
elements.sent-2411-976: The dependency of fine-tuning on existing data makes it less useful, especially when considering new writing styles, or newly developed data structures or ontologies are preferred.
elements.sent-2411-977: In this project, we explore the potential of LLMs in structured data extraction without fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-978: Specifically, given an ontology (formally defined concepts and relationships) for organic synthesis, we aim to extract structured information as Resource Description Framework (RDF) graphs from unstructured text using LLMs with zero-shot prompting.

elements.section-2411-033: Knowledge Graph RAG for Polymer Simulation
elements.sent-2411-1001: Authors: Jiale Shi, Weijie Zhang, Dandan Tang, Chi Zhang.
elements.figure-2411-041: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{images/PicturePolymerRAG.png} \caption{Creating Knowledge Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation (KGRAG) for Polymer Simulation. \label{fig:polymerRAG}}
elements.sent-2411-1002: Molecular modeling and simulations have become essential tools in polymer science and engineering, offering predictive insights into macromolecular structure, dynamics, and material properties.
elements.sent-2411-1003: However, the complexity of polymer simulations poses challenges in model development/selection, computational method choices, advanced sampling techniques, and data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-1004: While literature \cite{Gartner2019 provides guidelines to ensure the validity and reproducibility of these simulations, these resources are often presented in massive, unstructured text formats, making it difficult for new learners to systematically and comprehensively understand the correct approaches for model selection, simulation execution, and post-processing analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-1005: Therefore, this study presents a novel approach to address these challenges by implementing a Knowledge Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation (KGRAG) system for building an AI chatbot focused on polymer simulation guidance and education.
elements.sent-2411-1006: Our team utilized the large language model GPT-3.5 Turbo \cite{OpenAI2024b and Microsoft's GraphRAG \cite{GraphRAG2024, Edge2024 framework to extract polymer simulation-related entities and relationships from unstructured documents, constructing a comprehensive KGRAG, as shown in \autoref{fig:polymerRAG , where the nodes are colored by their degrees.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1007: Those nodes with high degrees include “Polymer Simulation”, “Atomistic Model,” “CG Model,” “Force Field,” and “Polymer Informatics,” which are all the keywords about polymer simulation and modeling, illustrating the effective performance of entity extraction.
elements.sent-2411-1008: We run the query engineering of KGRAG to ask questions.
elements.sent-2411-1009: For comparative analysis, we also implemented a baseline Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system using LlamaIndex \cite{LlamaIndex2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-1010: Upon comparing the responses from the baseline RAG and KGRAG by human experts, we found that the KGRAG demonstrates substantial improvements in question-answering performance when analyzing complex and high-level information about polymer simulation.
elements.sent-2411-1011: This improvement is attributed to KGRAG's ability to capture semantic concepts and uncover hidden connections within the data, providing more accurate, logical, and insightful responses compared to traditional vector-based RAG methods.
elements.sent-2411-1012: This study contributes to the growing field of data-driven approaches in polymer science by offering a powerful tool for knowledge extraction and synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-1013: Our KGRAG system shows promise in enhancing the understanding of massive unstructured polymer simulation guidance in the relevant literature, potentially improving the validity and reproducibility of these polymer simulations, and accelerating the development of new polymer simulation methods.
elements.sent-2411-1014: We found that the quality of prompts is crucial for effective entity extraction and knowledge graph construction.
elements.sent-2411-1015: Therefore, our future work will focus on optimizing prompts for entity extraction, relationship building, and knowledge graph construction to further improve the system's performance and applicability in polymer simulation research.

elements.section-2411-034: Synthetic Data Generation and Insightful Machine Learning for High Entropy Alloy Hydrides
elements.sent-2411-1020: Authors: Tapashree Pradhan, Devi Dutta Biswajeet The generation of synthetic data in materials science and chemistry is traditionally performed by machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) that approximate the first principle functional form used to compute wave functions of known electronic configurations \cite{Focassio2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-1021: Synthetic data is a component of the active-learning feedback loop that is utilized to retrain these potentials.
elements.sent-2411-1022: The purpose of this active-learning component is to incorporate a wider range of physical conditions into the potential’s application domain.
elements.sent-2411-1023: However, the initial cost of data generation to train the MLIPs is a major setback for complex chemistries like in the case of high entropy alloys (HEAs).
elements.sent-2411-1024: The potential application of high entropy alloys in hydrogen storage \cite{Marques2021 demands acceleration in the computation of surface and mechanical properties of the alloys by better approximation of the potential landscape.}
elements.sent-2411-1025: The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in the generation of synthetic data to tackle this bottleneck problem poses an alternative to the traditional MLIPs.
elements.sent-2411-1026: LLMs like ChatGPT, Llama3, Claude, and Gemini \cite{Jain2022 learn from text-embeddings of the training data, capturing inherent trends between semantic or numerical relationships of the text and making them suitable for learning certain complex relationships in materials physics that might be present in the language itself.}
elements.sent-2411-1027: The current work aims to build LLM applications working in conjunction with an external database of high entropy alloy hydrides via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) \cite{Lewis2020b to populate synthetic data for predictive modeling later.}
elements.sent-2411-1028: The inbuilt RAG feature of GPT-4 enables us to write a few prompts to make a synthetic data generator utilizing a custom dataset of HEA hydrides.
elements.sent-2411-1029: The work also utilizes OpenAI’s API \cite{OpenAI2020 and the text-embedding-3-large model \cite{OpenAI2023 to configure custom generators that can be fine-tuned via prompts for synthetic data generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-1030: The development of the entire product is aimed at a web-based application that allows users to upload their datasets and instruct the GPT model to generate more entries that can serve as training data for predictive ML models like hydrogen capacity predictors.
elements.sent-2411-1031: The term “Insightful Machine Learning” refers to a sequential pipeline starting with (a) a reference database that serves as the retrieval component of an LLM, (b) the generation of synthetic data and features, and (c) getting insights from a chatbot on physics of the problem having multiple retrieval components inclusive of the predictive model.
elements.sent-2411-1032: The current code to generate synthetic data is available for use and modification.
elements.sent-2411-1033: The proposed pipeline shown in Figure 1 requires a validation stage which is essential for active learning.
elements.sent-2411-1034: The ongoing work involves the search for novel validation techniques taking inspiration from recently published works on information theory.
elements.sent-2411-1035: The future direction of the work is to complete the validation phase and have a product utilizing the pipeline for high entropy hydrides that can accelerate the search and discovery process without performing complex first principle calculations for data generation and training.
elements.sent-2411-1036: Generative AI models are revolutionizing molecular design by learning from vast chemical datasets to create novel compounds \cite{Bilodeau2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-1037: The challenge in molecular design goes beyond just creating new structures.
elements.sent-2411-1038: A key issue is ensuring the designed molecules have specific useful properties, e.g., high solubility, high synthesizability, etc. LLMs that can be conditioned on the desired property seem to be a promising solution \cite{Jablonka2024d .}
elements.sent-2411-1039: However, current frontier models often lack an innate chemical understanding, which can lead to impractical or even dangerous molecular designs \cite{Mirza2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-1040: A factor that distinguishes many successful chemists is their chemical intuition.
elements.sent-2411-1041: This intuition, for instance, describes the preference for certain compounds that are not grounded in knowledge that can be easily conveyed but rather by tacit knowledge accumulated over years of experience.
elements.sent-2411-1042: If models could possess this chemical intuition, they would be more useful for real-world scientific applications.
elements.sent-2411-1043: In this work, we introduce ChemSense, in which we explore how well frontier models are aligned with human experts in chemistry.
elements.sent-2411-1044: By aligning AI with human knowledge and preferences, one can aim to create tools that can propose feasible, safe, and desirable molecular structures, bridging the gap between theoretical capabilities and practical scientific needs.
elements.sent-2411-1045: Moreover, ChemSense would help us in understanding the emergent alignment of frontier models with dataset scale and size.
elements.sent-2411-1046: Yang et al.\ (2024) investigated the ability of LLMs to predict human characteristics in their social life and showed that LLMs encountered great difficulties in predicting these.
elements.sent-2411-1047: Mirza et al.\ (2024) benchmarked LLMs on various chemistry tasks; however, good performance in that benchmark does not guarantee alignment with human-expert intuitions.
elements.sent-2411-1048: Chennakesavalu et al.\ (2024) aligned LLMs to generate low energy stable molecules with externally specified properties, they noticed huge improvement upon alignment compared to the base model.

elements.sent-2411-1068: To address this challenge, we developed GlossaGen, an innovative framework that leverages large language models to automatically generate glossaries from PDF or TeX files, streamlining the process for academics.
